coredata.dc:title: Anticrossing spectroscopy in multi-nanolayer structures
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Makarov, Vladimir I.
Khmelinskii, Igor
coredata.dc:description: Presently we explored nanosandwich structures with graphite (Gt) and graphene (Gn) nanolayers. We found that in Pt–SiO2–Gt, Pt–BN–Gt and Pt–SiO2–Ni–Gn structures the spectra may be decomposed into several components, each corresponding to a different value of the total spin angular momentum S. Only one component was required to describe the Pt–SiO2–Ni–Gn spectra at 5.3K, with additional components appearing at higher temperatures. On the other hand, a single component described the Pt–BN–Ni–Gn spectra at all temperatures. Temperature dependence of the spectra of the Pt–SiO2–Ni–Gn system was studied in the 5.3–75.3K range. Presently we obtained experimental results for novel sandwich systems, with the Gn layer only two monoatomic layers thick. Thus, we compared experimental spectra of a three-nanolayer sandwich system containing a Gt nanolayer with those of a four-nanolayer system containing a diatomic Gn layer. The experimental results were discussed using a theoretical model of the respective physical mechanisms. We propose an exchange anticrossing mechanism, whereby the spin-state polarization of the given Zeeman׳s substate in the Pt nanolayer is transported to Gt or Ni–Gn nanolayer by the exchange interaction between the two layers. As long as exchange interaction coupling spin states in different nanolayers is involved, we term the respective spectra the “spin anticrossing exchange-resonance spectra”. This clarifies the physical origins of some of the model parameters, i.e. the growing external magnetic field shifts the Zeeman׳s substates in the different layers differently, producing the anticrossing spectrum. In the frameworks of the developed model, we propose spin–orbit (SO) interaction as the main factor inducing the spin–lattice relaxation, which is one of the important factors determining the line shape. We performed ab initio calculations of the SO interaction in carbon and metal nanolayers, finding that the SO interactions monotonously increase with the atomic number.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Interfaces
B. Magnetic materials
C. Semiconductor
D. Magnetic properties
E. Spin-density waves

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Efficiency of intra-nanolayer spin-polarized state transport
Intra-layer interactions coupling continuum and discrete states

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Raman spectrum of the graphite nanolayer deposited in 5min on top of the SiO2 nanolayer.
Raman spectrum of graphene deposited on top the SiO2–Ni sample.
Raman spectrum of the nanostructured BN material obtained by PLD from a solid BN pellet.
Typical exchange anti-crossing resonance spectrum for Pt (8.5nm)–SiO2 (7.9nm)–Gt (8.7nm) three-nanolayer structure.
Exchange anticrossing resonance spectra of (a) Pt (8.6 nm)–SiO2 (7.7nm)–Ni (8.1nm)–Gn (bilayer) device recorded at 75.3 and 5.3K; and (b) control spectrum without graphene.
Exchange anticrossing resonance spectrum recorded at 77K for the Pt (8.6nm)–BN nanomesh (6.3nm)–Gt (7.5nm) structure.

Exchange anticrossing resonance spectrum recorded for the (a) Pt (8.6nm)–BN nanomesh (6.3nm)–Ni (8.3nm)–Gn (bilayer) structure at 77 K; (b) control experiment spectrum without graphene; and (c) simulated spectrum (see the Phenomenological Approach section); note that the simulated line intensities decay much faster than the experimental spectrum.

parameters for each of the spectral components (a)-(d).

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, University of Puerto Rico, Rio Piedras, P.O. Box 23343, San Juan, PR 00931-3343, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, University of Puerto Rico

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Universidade do Algarve, FCT, DQF, and CIQA, P8005-139 Faro, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Universidade do Algarve, FCT, DQF, and CIQA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Presently we explored nanosandwich structures with graphite (Gt) and graphene (Gn) nanolayers. We found that in Pt–SiO2–Gt, Pt–BN–Gt and Pt–SiO2–Ni–Gn structures the spectra may be decomposed into several components, each corresponding to a different value of the total spin angular momentum S. Only one component was required to describe the Pt–SiO2–Ni–Gn spectra at 5.3K, with additional components appearing at higher temperatures. On the other hand, a single component described the Pt–BN–Ni–Gn spectra at all temperatures. Temperature dependence of the spectra of the Pt–SiO2–Ni–Gn system was studied in the 5.3–75.3K range. Presently we obtained experimental results for novel sandwich systems, with the Gn layer only two monoatomic layers thick. Thus, we compared experimental spectra of a three-nanolayer sandwich system containing a Gt nanolayer with those of a four-nanolayer system containing a diatomic Gn layer. The experimental results were discussed using a theoretical model of the respective physical mechanisms. We propose an exchange anticrossing mechanism, whereby the spin-state polarization of the given Zeeman׳s substate in the Pt nanolayer is transported to Gt or Ni–Gn nanolayer by the exchange interaction between the two layers. As long as exchange interaction coupling spin states in different nanolayers is involved, we term the respective spectra the “spin anticrossing exchange-resonance spectra”. This clarifies the physical origins of some of the model parameters, i.e. the growing external magnetic field shifts the Zeeman׳s substates in the different layers differently, producing the anticrossing spectrum. In the frameworks of the developed model, we propose spin–orbit (SO) interaction as the main factor inducing the spin–lattice relaxation, which is one of the important factors determining the line shape. We performed ab initio calculations of the SO interaction in carbon and metal nanolayers, finding that the SO interactions monotonously increase with the atomic number.

ce:section.ce:para: Spintronics involve active manipulation of spin degrees of freedom in solid-state systems. The control of spin is then a control of either population and phase of spins in an ensemble of particles, or a coherent spin manipulation in single- or multiple-spin systems. The goals of spintronics are to study interactions between spin and its solid-state environment and produce useful spintronic devices. Thus fundamental studies focus on spin transport, dynamics and relaxation.

One of the most important problems in spintronics is generation of spin-polarized states, usually by creating a nonequilibrium spin population. Spin can be oriented using optical techniques by transferring angular momenta of circularly-polarized photons to electrons, although electrical spin injection is more desirable for practical applications. However, the rates of spin accumulation depend on spin relaxation rates, in both cases. The mechanisms of spin relaxation have been analyzed earlier [1–8]. Typical spin relaxation time scales are in the pico- to microsecond time range. Spin detection is also important. The common goal in many spintronic devices is to maximize the spin-polarized state generation and detection sensitivity. Earlier we proposed a novel device to generate and detect spin-polarized states [4–8], using exchange state-anticrossing phenomenon.

State anticrossing is quite well known in quantum physics, investigated for example in intermediate-case molecules [1–3]. Earlier we reported on spin-polarized state transport between two conductive nanolayers, separated by an insulator [4–8], interpreted as resulting from the exchange anticrossing resonance. Phenomenological models were proposed and validated [4–8], without discussing the underlying physical mechanisms in detail.

The results in three-nanolayer metal-dielectric-semiconductor (MDS) devices were interpreted using phenomenological models and ab initio quantum chemical calculations [8]. We found that the accuracy of the ab initio methods is sufficient, being able to reproduce the structure of exchange anticrossing resonance spectra. The ab initio calculations acceptably reproduce the spectral line positions and numbers in the experimental spectra, with encouraging prospects for future usage of this theoretical approach.

Ferromagnetism in different semiconductor–transition metal systems is a very interesting phenomenon, which may find spintronic applications. This phenomenon, however, has not been explored either experimentally or theoretically in nanolayers. Note that according to ab initio analysis at high temperatures, ferromagnetism was predicted to occur in GaN doped with transition metals. Theoretically, such materials can lead to semiconductor-based spintronic applications [9–11]. Spintronic devices have more functionality in comparison with conventional semiconductors, using both electrical charge and spin of the electrons. Ferromagnetism in semiconductors diluted by atoms of transition elements at room temperature was reported earlier [12]. Magnetic doping is usually limited by solubility of transition metals in semiconductors, as was the case of GaN doped by Mn [13,14]. Ferromagnetism may be obtained with 3d and some 4d non-magnetic dopants [15–17]. For example, properties of Ag and Cd dopants in ZnO have been considered theoretically earlier [18,19]. It has also been shown that Pd atoms in GaN are ferromagnetically ordered [20,21].

In the present study, we focus our attention on detailed experimental investigation of three- and four-nanolayer Pt–SiO2–graphite (PSGt), Pt–SiO2–Ni–graphene (PSNGn), Pt–BN nanomesh–graphite (PBGt) and Pt–BN nanomesh–Ni–graphene (PBNGn) devices. All of these devices were experimentally tested for spin-polarized state transport from the Pt nanolayer to Gt or Ni–Gn nanolayer, obtaining experimental data on the effects of diatomic graphene layers on spin transport.

Our goals include recording of the exchange anticrossing resonance spectra for the new devices, where the metal or semiconductor nanolayer in the output section of the device was substituted by either Gt or Gn. Another important goal is to continue development of the theory of exchange anticrossing resonances observed in nanolayer sandwich devices, along with analysis of the physical mechanisms behind the earlier introduced phenomenological models [4–8]. The developed theoretic approach was used to interpret, analyze and simulate the experimentally recorded spectra, advancing the main goal of the present studies.

The presently used experimental setup has been described in detail earlier [4–7]. In short, it included a home-made device, built around a solid-state sandwich structure, deposited on a (100) Si substrate (commercial product, 0.15mm thickness). A partial cross-section of a typical sandwich structure has been published earlier [4,5]. Charge sputtering was used to deposit the metal layers, and laser vapor deposition was used to deposit the dielectric layer. Graphite and graphene layers were deposited in a tube-furnace reactor [22–25]. In particular, graphite nanolayer of controllable thickness was deposited on top of the SiO2 nanolayer of the previously prepared Si–Pt–SiO2 sandwich. The temperature in the tube-furnace reactor was increased from 25°C to 950°C at 10−3 Torr; next an H2+5% CH4 gas mixture was passed through the reactor at 200Torr total pressure and 100ccm gas flow during a certain time, providing layer growth at about 1.3nm/min rate, for at least 5min. A typical Raman spectrum of the deposited graphite nanolayer is shown in Fig. 1. Raman spectra were recorded using a home-made Raman spectrometer assembled from commercial components as described in [26], with the Ar ion laser operating at 514.5nm used as the radiation source.
Graphene was deposited on SiO2 with an intermediate Ni catalyst nanolayer 8.7nm thick using the method proposed earlier [27]. Raman spectrum of the deposited material is shown in Fig. 2.
Note that the recorded spectrum corresponds with good accuracy to that of a graphene bilayer [28].

The two devices of Pt–BN–Gt and Pt–BN–Ni–Gn compositions were produced using the same methods. The BN-nanomesh layer was deposited on top of the Pt layer using Pulsed Laser Deposition. A solid BN target was evaporated by KrF excimer laser pulses (Lambda Physics, LPX-200) at ~10−7 Torr, covering the Si–Pt target. The average pulse energy and repetition rate were was 370mJ/pulse and 10Hz, respectively. Typical Raman spectrum of the deposited BN nanomesh is shown in Fig. 3. The Raman spectrum reveals two characteristic bands at 803 and 1356cm−1, typical for hexagonal nanostructured BN. The A 2u (805cm−1) mode is close to 803cm−1 and the E 1v (1367cm−1) mode is close to 1356cm−1 mode of the bulk material [29–33].
The layer thickness was verified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on cross-cut samples, prepared using heavy-ion milling.

The device included a ferrite needle (TPS&TPSA, Power Electronics Technology), with the needle tip 50μm in diameter, made of a stainless steel capillary filled by ferrite powder suspended in glycerol, and the body 1mm in diameter. A spiral coil of copper wire (0.3mm wire diameter, 10 turns) was wound on the needle body. The needle tip touched the surface of a Si substrate at the (100) plane. A second ferrite item (TPS&TPSA, Power Electronics Technology), with the input surface 10mm in diameter and the body 1mm in diameter, contacted the output metal surface by way of a magnetic contact provided by ferrite powder suspended in glycerol (1:1w/w) (TPS&TPSA, Power Electronics Technology, 25μm average particle diameter). Copper wire, 0.3mm in diameter, was wound on the body of the item (10 turns). Note that the same high-frequency ferrite material was used everywhere, rated for up to 100MHz applications. The entire assembly with the nanosandwich sample was placed into a liquid nitrogen bath (T≈77K), to reduce noise.

A home-made current generator, with the electronic circuit shown in Fig. 1(b), was controlled via an I/O data acquisition board (National Instruments DAQ, PCI-6034E), which was programmed in the LABVIEW environment and run on a Dell PC. The generator fed pulsed output currents of up to 10A into the input coil. The pulse shape was programmed to reproduce the linear function,
(1)
I
2
( t ) =
{
0 ,

0
I

∈ 0
) / τ ,

≤ t <
t
0
+ τ
0 ,
t
0
+ τ ≤ t
where I 0, t 0 and τ (pulse amplitude, start time and duration) could be programmed. The output coil was connected to a digital oscilloscope (LeCroy; WaveSurfer 432), which collected and averaged the output signal. The I/O DAQ board generated the analog signal controlling the current generator and produced a rectangular TTL pulse, 100ns in duration, triggering the oscilloscope with its rising edge, 100ns before the start of the analog control signal sweep. The magnetic field sweep rate was 0.684kG/μs (the maximum field created at 10μs scan duration is 6.84kG).
Experiments were typically made at 77K. Low-temperature measurements were carried out at 5.3K using a closed-cycle liquid He cryostat (ARS; model CS202⁎E-DMX-1AL).
In the present study we tested several multilayer sandwich structures: Pt–SiO2–graphite (PSGt), Pt–SiO2–Ni–graphene (PSGn), Pt–BN nanomesh–graphite (PBGt) and Pt–BN nanomesh–Ni–graphene (PBNGn).
All measurements were performed at 77K. A typical exchange anti-crossing resonance spectrum for this structure is shown in Fig. 4.

The spectrum in Fig. 4 is quite complex, and different from the earlier obtained spectra [4–8]. It was represented as a superposition of three independent components consisting of 15, 7 and 5 lines, respectively, each of the components fitted using the earlier developed phenomenological approach [7,8]. The values of phenomenological parameters ΔE 12,0, Δg 12 and τ 12 were obtained, where Δg 12 is the difference of the g-factors of unpaired electrons located in the Pt and Gt nanolayers, and 1 /
τ
12
= 1 / 2

+ 1 / 2

τ

τ
2
are spin–lattice relaxation times of unpaired electrons in the two conductive nanolayers. Several structures of this composition were prepared, with different thickness of the Gt nanolayer. The values of the respective fitting parameters are listed in Table 1.

The thickness-dependent parameters were fitted by an empirical relation

where i=1, 2, and 3 correspond to the three spectral components. The values of the fitting parameters according to Eq. (2) are listed in Table 2.

Using Eq. (2) and the results of Table 2, we estimated Δg 12 and (τ 12)−1 values for very thick graphite layers, h
Gt,i →∞. These values are also listed in Table 2 under Δg 12,i (∞) and [τ 12,i (∞)]−1, respectively.

The exchange anticrossing resonance spectra of this structure were recorded in the range from 5.3K to 75.3K with 10K step. The respective spectra recorded at 75.3 and 5.3K are plotted in Fig. 5. In this figure, the spectrum of the control device with Pt (8.6nm)–SiO2 (7.7nm)–Ni (8.1nm) structure is also shown.

We see that the spectra become simpler at lower temperatures, while the linewidths become smaller. These spectra were decomposed into three independent components, characterized by different temperature dependences. The three simulated components (a, b, c) appear in the spectrum measured at 75.3K.

The line amplitudes in the spectral component (c) were temperature-independent, while the line width temperature dependence was described by
(3)

using wavenumber units for the activation energy in the exponential. Both amplitudes and widths of the spectral component (b) depend on temperature:

Similarly, the spectral component (a) is also temperature-dependent

The structure of the spectral components was interpreted using the previously developed phenomenological model [4–8] in the high-spin limit for components (b) and (c), with the total spin S=23/2 (b) and S=13 (c), and in the low-spin limit for the component (a), with S=27/2. We conclude that the components (a) and (b) are thermally activated, while only the line width is activated for the component (c). The latter may be explained by thermal activation of the spin–lattice relaxation, which affects the line width as previously discussed [4–8]. Using the phenomenological model, we estimated Δg 12 value and determined the temperature dependences of the effective spin–lattice relaxation rate

. The values of the respective parameters are listed in Table 3.

A control experiment with a three-nanolayer Pt (8.6nm)–SiO2 (7.7nm)–Ni (8.1nm) structure was carried out at 75.3K ( see Fig. 5b). This spectrum may be decomposed into two spectral components, equivalent to components (b) and (c) discussed above. Therefore, the component (a) only appears in the presence of the graphene bilayer.

We tested a Pt (8.6nm)–BN nanomesh (6.3nm)–Gt (7.5nm) sandwich structure, with the spectrum shown in Fig. 6. This spectrum may be decomposed into 4 spectral components, which were deconvoluted using a standard procedure.

Using the same phenomenological model [4–8], we estimated the values of the Δg 12 and
(
τ
12
)
− 1
parameters, listed in Table 4.
We tested a Pt (8.6nm)–BN nanomesh (6.3nm)–Ni (8.3nm)–Gn (bilayer) structure at 77K, with the respective exchange anticrossing resonance spectrum shown in Fig. 7.

The spectrum was interpreted using the same phenomenological model; it has 26 lines, corresponding to the total spin S=51/2 [4–8]. Values of the parameters ΔE 12,0, Δg 12 and
(
τ
12
)
− 1
are 0.135cm−1, 1.71 and 5.27µs−1, respectively.

Similarly to the previous system, we made control experiments with a three-nanolayer Pt (8.6nm)–BN nanomesh (6.3nm)–Ni (8.3nm) structure at 77K. The respective spectrum (shown in Fig. 7b) presents the same line system as that in Fig. 8a, although at a considerably lower signal-to-noise ratio. Thus, this time the only observable effect of the graphene bilayer is an amplification of the spectral lines.

The earlier proposed phenomenological model [4–8] will be used to analyze the anticrossing spectra and will be partially reproduced here. The model assumes that magnetic moment is transported between two conductive nanolayers of different materials, separated by a dielectric nanolayer. The detected signal appears due to the difference of the electron g-factors and spin-state populations with the same M
S quantum number between the two nanolayers. Here, M
S is the projection of the total spin on the external magnetic field direction. The population difference is generated by the zero-field energy difference ΔE 0 between the states in the two conductive nanolayers
n
1
n
2
=
e
− Δ
E
0
/
k
B
T
Note that the ratio between the relaxation rate of the Zeeman׳s sublevels and the magnetic field increase rate is also an important model parameter. Thus, the state population difference between the two materials creates strongly non-equilibrium conditions, causing a fast spin transfer from the input layer to the output layer, when the Zeeman׳s sub-states in the two layers are in resonance. The relative shift of the levels in external magnetic field is due to the difference of the material g-factors in different layers. Such transitions are induced by interlayer exchange interaction, as we proposed earlier [4–8] and will apply presently. This is the main mechanism of interlayer transport of spin-polarized states. Another important process, developing simultaneously, is intra-layer spin–lattice relaxation. Relaxation may be induced by spin–orbit, electron–phonon, and other interactions. The mechanisms of g-factor formation and spin–lattice relaxation were partially considered earlier [6–8]. Lastly, we have to include intra-layer spin-polarized state transfer, which we have not considered before.

Here, we shall analyze the mechanisms of inter-nanolayer magnetic moment transfer and intra-nanolayer spin–lattice relaxation, which were not discussed in detail before. Our model is built around a system of two coupled levels 1 and 2, which are Zeeman sublevels in the two nanolayers with the same M
S value; g
i , i=1, 2 is the electron g-factor in nanolayers 1 and 2; E
i0 is zero-field energy of the two states, dependent on the composition and thickness of the two nanolayers; γ
i is the state width, dependent on the relaxation rates of state population, W
i is the intra-nanolayer spin-polarized transfer rate, depending on intra-layer interactions. The magnetic field sweep rate W
m is directly proportional to the input current sweep rate of the device a
d

where t is time. The analysis of our phenomenological model is given in Appendix I, and the final relationship obtained there is presented as follows:

The relationship (7) may be represented as follows (see Appendix I):

. This is acceptable for qualitative analysis, as the g
i parameters in (7) affect the peak amplitudes but not the peak positions. As both
ρ

ρ

( t ) exhibit resonances simultaneously, the same resonances appear in the output signal (8) [4–8]. Most of the parameters appearing in Eqs. (7) and (8) were discussed earlier [6–8], apart from parameters b 10 and b 20 (see Appendix I), which will be analyzed here.

Taking into account the parameters determined above for the Pt (8.6nm)–BN nanomesh (6.3nm)–Ni (8.3nm)–Gn (bilayer) device (S=51/2; ΔE 12,0, Δg 12 and
(
τ
12
)
− 1
parameters are respectively 0.135cm−1, 1.71 and 5.27µs−1), and the parameters of the experimental setup (see Section 2), we simulated the experimental spectrum of Fig. 7a. The respective simulated spectrum is shown in Fig. 7c. The variable parameters in the simulation were a, b 10, and b 20. Perfect agreement between experimental and simulated spectra was obtained for the following values of the variable parameters: 0.037cm–1, 0.067cm–1 and 0.059cm–1. These values are quite reasonable, corresponding respectively to 1.11×109, 2.01×109 and 1.77×109 Hz on the frequency scale. Note that all of these parameters are in the GHz range, as is expectable for solids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficiency of intra-nanolayer spin-polarized state transport
ce:section.ce:para: We estimated the parameters b 10 and b 20 for the Pt (8.6nm)–BN nanomesh (6.3nm)–Ni (8.3nm)–Gn (bilayer) device. Note that the phenomenological parameters b 10 and b 20 describe the efficiency of spin-polarized state intra-nanolayer transfer. Fig. 9 shows a diagram of the energy levels involved.

Here, two cases are possible, requiring separate analysis: (a) the closest occupied discrete level lies above the Fermi level and (b) the closest vacant discrete level lies below the Fermi level. We take into accounts the thermal distribution of electrons over the zone, described by Fermi statistics [34]. The parameter V describes the interaction between the two level systems, causing transitions. Additionally, we take into account Zeeman׳s splitting of the discrete spectrum levels. The transitions are only calculated to or from the discrete level closest to the Fermi level (see Fig. 9a and b). The energy gap between the Fermi level and the Zeeman׳s sublevels of the discrete spectrum is dependent on the magnetic field.

We shall represent each of the b
i0 parameters as a sum of two components that will be deduced separately
(9)
b
i 0
=
b
i 1
+
b
i 2
We will analyze cases (a) and (b) separately, assuming that electron transfer from filled levels in the Fermi zone to a discrete level, or vice versa.

The electron transfer rate from a discrete level to an entire continuum spectrum is given by the golden Fermi rule [7,8]
(10)
W
D C
= 2
∫
ε
min
∞
|
V
D C
( ε )
|
2
δ ( ε ) d ε
where ε
min is the minimum energy of a vacant level in the zone, and |
V ( ε )
| is the absolute value of the interaction matrix element between discrete and continuum states. The latter equation may be rewritten
(11)
k
D C
=
2 π
ℏ
ρ ¯
C
|
V ¯
D C
|
2
where
ρ ¯
C
is the average state density in the continuum spectrum.
To analyze the electron transfer from filled levels of the continuum to a discrete state, we will use the same model shown in Fig. 9.

The total number of levels of the continuum within the energy interval Δε is ρ ( ε ) Δ ε . With the f(ε,T,t) function describing the population distribution, we obtain ρ ( ε ) f ( ε , T , t ) Δ ε = ρ
׳
11
. In equilibrium the population distribution is described by the Fermi-Dirac function
f
e q
( ε , T , t ) =
f
F D
( ε , T ) . In the absence of equilibrium, the distribution function may be acceptably described by
(12)
f ( ε , T , t ) =
f
F D
( ε , T ) φ ( t )
where
f
F D
( ε , T ) is the Fermi-Dirac state distribution function and φ ( t ) is a time-dependent factor, obtained by solving the system of equations for the density matrix (see Appendix II). We need the ρ
׳
11
and ρ
׳
22
matrix elements for our analysis (see Appendix II). They are a function of the various system parameters, including the population of spin-polarized states in the dense and discrete spectra of the nanolayer of interest. The entire set of parameters is obtained in the Appendix II. The previously introduced parameter b
i1 is given by
(13)
b
i 1
=
(
1
ρ
׳
11
( t )
d ρ
׳
11
( t )
d t
)
t → 0
In the frameworks of a phenomenological model, the transition probability from the discrete spectrum to the continuum with ρ ( ε ) ⪢ 1 is proportional to
( ρ ( ε ) )
− 1
[35]. The density of states in the continuum is large, therefore we obtain
(14)
b
i 1
⪡
b
i 2
Thus, we can write
(15)
b
i 0
= −
b
i 2
= −
2 π
ℏ
ρ ¯
C
|
V
D C
|
2
i.e. intra-layer spin-state dynamics is reduced to an irreversible transition from the discrete to the continuum spectrum. In this case, Eq. (8) may be rewritten as follows:
(16)
U

=
μ

In presence of time-dependent magnetic field, the energy difference ΔE(H) between the two states involved will also vary with time
(17)
Δ E ( H ( t ) ) = Δ
E
0
±
μ
B
(
g
1
−

2
)
M
S
H ( t )
Thus, Eq. (16) may be used for the analysis of the experimental exchange anticrossing resonance spectra. Coming back to the experimental spectrum simulation, we may now assign values of 0.067cm−1 and 0.059cm−1 to b 12 and b 22, determined by the golden Fermi rule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intra-layer interactions coupling continuum and discrete states
ce:section.ce:para: We already discussed the nature of interactions that may couple continuum and discrete states [6–8]. Presently, we shall only discuss the spin–orbit (SO) interaction.

The nature of spin–orbit interactions was extensively discussed earlier [36–38]. Presently, we performed non-relativistic ab initio calculations of the matrix elements of the SO interactions
(18)
V
S O
= −
μ
B
Z
e
2
8 π
m
2
r
3
(
L ^
S ^
)
between |
C , J
M
J
L
M
L
S
M
S
〉 and |
D , J
M
J
L
M
L
S
M
S
〉 states of conductive monolayers containing 25 atoms, disposed in a square of 5×5 atoms; see Fig. 10. Note that the SO interaction is a relativistic phenomenon, but we are using an approximate non-relativistic approach, considering isotropic component of the magnetic dipole–dipole interaction between magnetic moments of spin and orbital angular momenta. The Hamiltonian of such interaction is given by Eq. (18), and the SO interaction energy is given by matrix elements of the non-relativistic wavefunctions of this Hamiltonian, according to an approximate expression
(19)
〈
V
S O
〉
= −
a
B
〈
C , J
M
J
L
M

M
S
|
1
2
r
3
(
L ^
S ^
)
|
D , J ׳ M
׳
J
L ׳ M
׳
L
S M
׳
S
〉
where
a
B
= −
μ
B
Z
e
2
/ 16 π
m
2
. The J ,
M
J
L ,
M
L
quantum numbers are undefined in monoatomic conductive layers. Therefore,
(20)
〈
V
S O
〉
= −
a
B
〈
C , L
M
L
|
L ^
2
r
3
|
D , L ׳ M
׳
L
〉
〈
S

S

S ^

S ׳ M
׳
S
〉
where
(21)
|
〈
S
M
S
|
S ^

S

S
+ 1
〉

=
|

=

=
M

Δ S = 0 ;
Δ
M
S
= 0 ,
± 1
Finally, matrix elements were averaged over spin
(22)
〈
V
S O
〉
= −
a
B
2
S ( S + 1 ) −
M
S
(
M
S
+ 1 )
+
M
S
3
〈
C , L
M
L
|
L ^
2
r
3
|
D , L ׳ M
׳
L
〉
The SO interaction matrix elements were calculated for mono-layers of C, Sc, Tl, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Ag, Pt, and Au atoms. The integrals were calculated in the following way:
a)
Ab initio calculations of the |
C , L
M
L
S
M
S
〉 and |
D , L
M
L
S
M
S
〉 electronic states of the planar square cluster containing 25 atoms (see Fig. 10) were carried out using Gaussian-2000 (ROCK Cluster System) commercial software package. At the first step of calculations, we used RHF method with the 6-31G(d) basis set [39]. At the second step, CCSD structural optimization was performed with the same basis set [39,40].
b)
The 〈
C , L
M
L
|
L ^
2
r
3
|
D , L ׳ M
׳
L
〉 matrix elements were calculated using homemade Fortran software that used |
C , L
M
L
S
M
S
〉 and |
D , L
M
L
S
M
S
〉 wavefunctions as input files,
r →
=
i →
x +
j →
y +
k →
z .

Note that the |
C , L
M
L
S
M
S
〉 and |
D , L
M
L
S
M
S
〉 wave functions are determined by the same function of |
r , L
M
L
S
M
S
〉 , where
|
C , L
M
L
S
M
S
〉
=
|
x , y ,
z
0
, L
M
L
S
M
S
〉
(x, y are variables; z 0=const. is the atomic radius, r 0) and
|
D , L
M
L
S
M
S
〉
=
|
x
0
,
y
0
, z , L
M
L
S
M
S
〉
. Since the results of calculations are dependent on the selected values of (x 0, y 0), the final results are presented as averages over 25 independent calculations for x 0=(2n−1)r 0, y 0=(2m−1)r 0, where n=1, 2,…,5 and m=1, 2,…,5 are varied independently; z is the variable coordinate.
The results are listed in Table 5.

Table 1 shows that the SO interaction increases with the atomic number. Note that we disregard the nuclear motion, i.e. the Franck–Condon (FC) factor Q =
〈
υ
x x
|
υ
z
〉
, which could be quite low, was taken as unity. The FC factor may in fact significantly reduce the coupling. As we already mentioned, the SO interaction determines the intra-layer spin–lattice relaxation efficiency for nanolayers of the respective element/material, thus the approximate spin–lattice relaxation rate may be estimated using the golden Fermi rule, with the FC factor taken as unity. However, we can estimate the FC factor, using experimental values of the respective relaxation time (τ
exp ), and the relaxation rate from the golden Fermi rule ((τ
GFR )−1): Q ≈ »
τ
G F R
/
τ
exp
. Even so, we leave a detailed discussion of this issue for a future publication. Here we only discussed the physical mechanism of intra-layer spin–lattice relaxation.

We discussed the state anticrossing exchange resonance both here and earlier [4–8]. Note that the state anticrossing resonance effects have been observed in other solid-state systems earlier [41–43]. These authors observed state anticrossing induced by N atoms placed in the InSb lattice, its physical nature different from that observed in our works. Note that authors [42,43] carried out theoretical studies of state anticrossing resonance in solid InN x Sb1−x , developing adequate theoretical tools for their analysis. We shall apply these theoretical tools to our nanolayer systems in variable magnetic fields, with the results to be reported in a future publication. Detailed theoretical analysis will be presented for the four-layer Pt–SiO2–Ni–Gn(bilayer) and Pt–BN–Ni–Gn(bilayer) sandwich structures and also for multilayer structures.

We found that Pt–SiO2–Gt and Pt–BN–Gt three-layer, and Pt–SiO2–Ni–Gn and Pt–BN–Ni–Gn four-layer structures demonstrate exchange anticrossing effect in variable magnetic fields, with spectra different from those obtained earlier for other structures [4–8]. We found that in Pt–SiO2–Gt, Pt–BN–Gt and Pt–SiO2–Ni–Gn structures the exchange anticrossing resonance spectra can be presented by a superposition of several components, each corresponding to a different total spin S value. On the other hand, a single component was sufficient for the Pt–BN–Ni–Gn structure. We introduced the physical mechanism of the exchange anticrossing resonances in such structures, which may be used to simulate the spectra and estimate some model parameters. The developed theory includes spin–lattice relaxation, which determines the Zeeman׳s sublevel widths. Thus, the developed theoretical approach may be successfully applied to the analysis of spin-exchange anticrossing resonance spectra for any three nano-layer sandwich devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: V. M. is very grateful for financial support from Grant no. DE-F602-o8ER46526.

ce:section.ce:para:
The magnetic field strength in the input section of sandwich device is given by
(AI.1)
H ( t ) l = N I ( t ) = N
a
d
t = H ( t ) N d
H ( t ) =
a
d
t
d
where l=Nd, N is the number of turns in the input section of the device, d is the wire diameter. The magnetic field flux induced in the output coil is given by
(AI.2)
Φ ( t ) = π
d
2
4
H ( t ) = π
d
a
d
t
4
Therefore, the potential induced in the output coil is given by
(AI.3)
d Φ ( t )
d t
= π
d
2
4
d H ( t )
d t
= π
d
a
d
4
= π
d
c
E
i n d
=
U
i n d
= c o n s t
where d
c is the coil diameter, E
ind is the induced electric field strength in the output coil and U
ind is the induced output potential. Therefore, no resonance peaks are to be detected in a classic system. However, the analysis of the quantum system using the two-level model reproduces the magnetic moment transfer between the two conductive nanolayers separated by dielectric nanolayer. The density matrix equation system

The system (AI.4) cannot be solved analytically; therefore, it was integrated numerically using the Runge–Kutta method. The output voltage is given by the time derivative of the magnetic flux

Spin-polarized state dynamics between discrete and continuum spectra is induced by intra nanolayer interactions. The equation system describing the dynamics may be written as follows:

In the frameworks of a phenomenological model, the probability of transition from the discrete spectrum to the continuum ρ ( ε ) ⪢ 1 is proportional to ( 1 / ρ ( ε ) )

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of quantum filters for selected spin polarized states
Spin-polarized state transport from ferromagnetic to conductive material: signal amplification by ferromagnetic layer
Photodissociation (32040–32090cm−1) of (SO2…XH) Van der Waals complexes and clusters (XH=C2H2, C2H4, C2H6) with formation of HSO2 and X
Spin polarized state quantum filter used to measure spin polarized state relaxation time and g-factor
Exchange resonance in MDM nanolayer systems: experiment and theory
Bottom-up fabrication of photoluminescent graphene quantum dots with uniform morphology

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M. Fabiane, S. Khamlich, A. Bello, J. Dangbegnon, D.Momodu, A.T.C. Johnson, N. Manyala, AIP Advances, 3 (2013) 112126, http://dx.doi.org/10.1063/1.4834975.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Philos. Trans. R. Soc. Lond. A

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: R.P. Feyman, Statistical Mechanics W.A. Benjiamin Inc., 1972.
Jens Hjörleifur Bárðarson, Effects of Spin-Orbit Coupling on Quantum Transport, geboren te Reykjavík, 1979.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin–spin and spin–orbit coupling studies of small species and magnetic system (Licentiate dissertation)

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M. Weyers, M. Sato, H. Arido, Jpn. J. Appl. Phys. Part 2, 31 (1992) 1853.
